content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good morning'? {{c1::おはようございます (Ohayō gozaimasu)}}
content.explanation:
This is the formal morning greeting. It is derived from the adjective 早い (hayai, meaning 'early'). The prefix お (o-) and suffix ございます (gozaimasu) elevate the phrase to polite speech (Keigo). For friends and family, the shortened informal おはよう (Ohayō) is used.

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: Imagine waking up early to find a giant glowing O-HAY stack (Ohayō) sitting in a formal courtroom in front of a judge.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good morning' en Japanese? おはようございます (Ohayō gozaimasu)
content.usage: Term: おはようございます (Ohayō gozaimasu) | Meaning: Good morning

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Hello / Good afternoon'? {{c1::こんにちは (Konnichiwa)}}
content.explanation:
Used as a general 'Hello' from late morning through afternoon. Historically, it was the start of a topic clause: Kon-nichi wa... (Today is...), where wa was followed by a wish. This is why the final sound is written with the hiragana particle は (ha) instead of わ (wa).

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: A huge clock pointing to noon (nichi) on a giant cone (kon), greeting everyone who passes by.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello / Good afternoon' en Japanese? こんにちは (Konnichiwa)
content.usage: Term: こんにちは (Konnichiwa) | Meaning: Hello / Good afternoon

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::こんばんは (Konbanwa)}}
content.explanation:
Used as a greeting when meeting someone in the evening. Similar to Konnichiwa, it was originally a topic clause: Kon-ban wa... (This evening is...), which is why the topic marker particle は (ha) is used to write the final 'wa' sound.

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: A giant cone (kon) holding a banjo (ban) under the starry evening sky, playing night tunes.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good evening' en Japanese? こんばんは (Konbanwa)
content.usage: Term: こんばんは (Konbanwa) | Meaning: Good evening

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Goodbye'? {{c1::さようなら (Sayōnara)}}
content.explanation:
A formal farewell that carries weight. It implies a long separation, similar to 'Farewell' or 'Adieu' in English, and is generally avoided in daily workplaces because it can sound like you won't see each other again. With classmates or coworkers, use informal parting phrases like mata ne or jā ne.

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: A traveler waving goodbye from a ship, throwing a giant SA-YO-NARA banner into the ocean.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye' en Japanese? さようなら (Sayōnara)
content.usage: Term: さようなら (Sayōnara) | Meaning: Goodbye

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::はじめまして (Hajimemashite)}}
content.explanation:
Derived from the verb 始める (hajimeru, meaning 'to begin' or 'to start'). It literally means 'it is beginning [for the first time]'. It is used strictly at the very beginning of a self-introduction when meeting someone for the first time.

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: Two runners at the start line (hajime) of a race, shaking hands and saying 'Nice to meet you' before the whistle blows.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en Japanese? はじめまして (Hajimemashite)
content.usage: Term: はじめまして (Hajimemashite) | Meaning: Nice to meet you

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good night'? {{c1::おやすみなさい (Oyasumi nasai)}}
content.explanation:
Used when parting in the late evening or when going to bed. Derived from the verb 休む (yasumu, meaning 'to rest' or 'to take a break'). The polite form adds the suffix なさい (nasai). For close friends and family, the casual おやすみ (Oyasumi) is standard.

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: A sleepy person resting in an O-YASU (oasis) at night, counting sheep that whisper 'nasai'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en Japanese? おやすみなさい (Oyasumi nasai)
content.usage: Term: おやすみなさい (Oyasumi nasai) | Meaning: Good night

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'See you / Bye - Informal'? {{c1::じゃあね (Jā ne)}}
content.explanation:
An extremely common, casual parting greeting used among close friends, family, and peers. じゃあ (Jā) is a contraction of dewa (well then), and ね (ne) is a friendly confirmation particle (like 'right?' or 'okay?').

🧠 Mnemonic Scene: Friends waving goodbye near a giant glass JAR (jā) filled with friendly smiley faces.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'See you / Bye - Informal' en Japanese? じゃあね (Jā ne)
content.usage: Term: じゃあね (Jā ne) | Meaning: See you / Bye - Informal

content.text: In Japanese, the general greeting 'Hello' used in the afternoon is '{{c1::こんにちは}}' (Konnichiwa).
content.explanation: This card emphasizes the spelling of the greeting. Although pronounced as 'wa', the final character is written using は (ha) because it acts as the grammatical topic marker particle. This stems from the phrase's historical origin as the beginning of a longer sentence: Kon-nichi wa [gokigen ikaga desu ka] (How are you doing today?).
content.spanish: En japonés, el saludo general 'Hola' en la tarde es 'こんにちは' (Konnichiwa).
content.usage: Term: こんにちは (Konnichiwa) | Use: Day greeting.

content.text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Good morning' is '{{c1::おはようございます}}' (Ohayou gozaimasu).
content.explanation: Represents the polite greeting used in the morning. In Japanese business culture, Ohayou gozaimasu is also used as the first greeting of the day when starting a shift, regardless of the actual time (even in the afternoon or night!).
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenos días' formalmente en japonés? おはようございます (Ohayou gozaimasu).
content.usage: Polite: おはようございます | Informal: おはよう

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::こんばんは}} (Konbanwa)
content.explanation: In this evening greeting, the final sound 'wa' is written as は (ha) because it was originally the topic particle in the clause: Kon-ban wa [ikaga osugoshi desu ka] (How is this evening treating you?). 今晩 (konban) means 'this evening' (今 = this, 晩 = evening).
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al llegar)' en japonés? こんばんは (Konbanwa).
content.usage: Term: こんばんは (Konbanwa)

content.text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Good night' (when going to bed) is '{{c1::おやすみなさい}}' (Oyasuminasai).
content.explanation: Formed by the prefix お (honorific), 休み (yasumi, rest/break), and なさい (polite command form). It literally translates to 'Please rest well'. Use おやすみ (Oyasumi) for children, peers, or family.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al despedirse o ir a dormir)' en japonés? おやすみなさい (Oyasuminasai).
content.usage: Polite: おやすみなさい | Informal: おやすみ

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally (implying a long separation)? {{c1::さようなら}} (Sayounara)
content.explanation: Historically derived from the phrase 左様ならば (sayō naraba, meaning 'if that is the case' or 'well then'). Over time, it became a standardized farewell. In daily contexts, it is best to use お疲れ様でした (Otsukaresama deshita, thank you for your hard work) with coworkers.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' formalmente en japonés? さようなら (Sayounara).
content.usage: Term: さようなら | Informal alternatives: またね (See you), じゃあね (Bye).

content.text: In Japanese, when meeting someone for the first time, you start with the greeting '{{c1::はじめまして}}' (Hajimemashite), which means 'Nice to meet you'.
content.explanation: Derived from 始めまして (from hajimeru - to begin). It signals that a new relationship is starting. Cultural note: It is always accompanied by a bow in Japan to show respect to the new acquaintance.
content.spanish: Al presentarse en japonés, se empieza con 'はじめまして' (Hajimemashite), que significa 'Encantado de conocerle'.
content.usage: Term: はじめまして (Hajimemashite)

content.text: In Japanese, at the end of a self-introduction, you say '{{c1::どうぞよろしくおねがいします}}' (Douzo yoroshiku onegaishimasu), which translates to 'Please be kind to me'.
content.explanation: A complex untranslatable phrase. どうぞ (douzo) means 'please/here you go', よろしく (yoroshiku) comes from yoi (good/favorable), and お願いします (onegaishimasu) means 'I make a request'. It literally means 'Please treat me favorably hereafter'.
content.spanish: Al final de una presentación en japonés, se dice 'どうぞよろしくおねがいします' (Douzo yoroshiku onegaishimasu), que equivale a 'Mucho gusto / Cuento con usted'.
content.usage: Term: どうぞよろしくおねがいします | Casual: よろしく

content.text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Please' (when asking for an action or object) is '{{c1::おねがいします}}' (Onegaishimasu) or '{{c2::ください}}' (Kudasai).
content.explanation: Difference in requests: お願いします (Onegaishimasu) is 'I pray/beg you' (comes from negau - to pray/request). ください (Kudasai) is 'give me' (polite form of kudaru - to hand down). You can say Onegaishimasu to order something in a restaurant or ask for help, but Kudasai is more transactional (e.g. 'Coffee, please' -> kōhī kudasai).
content.spanish: En japonés, para decir 'Por favor', se usa 'おねがいします' (Onegaishimasu) o 'ください' (Kudasai).
content.usage: Examples: お水をお願いします。 (Water, please.) | これ、ください。 (This one, please.)

content.text: In Japanese, the polite way to say 'Thank you very much' is '{{c1::ありがとうございます}}' (Arigatou gozaimasu).
content.explanation: Informally (with close peers), you can say just 'ありがとう' (Arigatou) or even 'どうも' (Doumo).
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Muchas gracias' en japonés? ありがとうございます (Arigatou gozaimasu).
content.usage: Polite: ありがとうございます | Informal: ありがとう

content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'You're welcome'? {{c1::どういたしまして}} (Douitashimashite)
content.explanation: The standard textbook response to 'Arigatou'. In natural conversation, speakers often say 'いいえ' (Iie - No problem).
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'De nada' en japonés? どういたしまして (Douitashimashite).
content.usage: Term: どういたしまして

content.text: In Japanese, how do you ask 'What is your name?' politely? {{c1::お名前は何ですか}} (O-namae wa nan desu ka)
content.explanation: The prefix 'o-' is honorific, expressing respect towards the other person.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cuál es su nombre?' en japonés? お名前は何ですか (O-namae wa nan desu ka).
content.usage: Dialogue: - お名前は何ですか。 - ジーザスです。

content.text: In Japanese, how do you ask 'How are you? / Are you well?'? {{c1::お元気ですか}} (O-genki desu ka)
content.explanation: Used when you haven't seen someone for a while. Not used daily like 'how are you' in English.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás? / ¿Está usted bien?' en japonés? お元気ですか (O-genki desu ka).
content.usage: Dialogue: - お元気ですか。 - はい、元気です。

content.text: In Japanese, '{{c1::すみません}}' (Sumimasen) is a highly useful word that means {{c2::Excuse me / Sorry / Thank you}}.
content.explanation: Used to get attention, apologize, or thank someone for going to trouble for you.
content.spanish: En japonés, 'すみません' (Sumimasen) significa 'Disculpe / Lo siento / Gracias' según el contexto.
content.usage: Example: すみません、ビルはどこですか？ (Excuse me, where is the building?)

content.scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'To be - Polite'? {{c1::です (desu)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Polite' en Japanese? です (desu)
content.usage: Term: です (desu) | Meaning: To be - Polite

content.scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'To not be - Polite'? {{c1::ではありません (dewa arimasen)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To not be - Polite' en Japanese? ではありません (dewa arimasen)
content.usage: Term: ではありません (dewa arimasen) | Meaning: To not be - Polite

content.scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Is it?'? {{c1::ですか (desu ka?)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Is it?' en Japanese? ですか (desu ka?)
content.usage: Term: ですか (desu ka?) | Meaning: Is it?

content.scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Japanese, how do you say 'I am a student'? {{c1::学生です (gakusei desu)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am a student' en Japanese? 学生です (gakusei desu)
content.usage: Term: 学生です (gakusei desu) | Meaning: I am a student

content.text: In Japanese, the standard polite word for 'I' is '{{c1::わたし}}' (Watashi).
content.explanation: In casual speech, males often use 'ぼく' (Boku) or 'おれ' (Ore). Females often use 'わたし' or 'あたし'.
content.spanish: En japonés, la palabra estándar y cortés para 'yo' es 'わたし' (Watashi).
content.usage: Polite: わたしはエンジニアです。 (I am an engineer.)

content.text: In Japanese, the word for 'you (singular)' is '{{c1::あなた}}' (Anata).
content.explanation: In Japanese culture, it is polite to address people by their name + '-san' rather than saying 'Anata' (which can sound overly direct or mean 'dear' when said by a spouse).
content.spanish: En japonés, 'tú' es 'あなた' (Anata), pero suele preferirse el nombre de la persona + 'san'.
content.usage: Rule: Prefer name + さん (san) over あなた. Example: マルコさんは学生ですか？ (Is Marco a student?)

content.text: In Japanese, the pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are '{{c1::かれ}}' (Kare) and '{{c2::かのじょ}}' (Kanojo).
content.explanation: 'Kanojo' also means 'girlfriend' and 'Kare' can mean 'boyfriend' depending on context.
content.spanish: En japonés, los pronombres para 'él' y 'ella' son 'かれ' (Kare) y 'かのじょ' (Kanojo).
content.usage: Examples: 彼は先生です。 (He is a teacher.) | 彼女は元気です。 (She is well.)

content.text: In Japanese, to make a pronoun plural, you add the suffix '{{c1::たち}}' (-tachi). For example, 'we' is '{{c2::わたしたち}}' (Watashitachi).
content.explanation: -tachi can be appended to personal pronouns or nouns representing people.
content.spanish: En japonés, para hacer plural un pronombre, se añade '-tachi'. 'Watashi' (yo) -> 'Watashitachi' (nosotros).
content.usage: Example: わたしたちは学生です。 (We are students.) | あなたたち (You all)

content.text: In Japanese, the polite copula meaning 'is / am / are' is '{{c1::です}}' (desu). Example: I am a student = 学生{{c2::です}}.
content.explanation: 'desu' is placed at the end of the sentence and serves as the predicate of state or identity.
content.spanish: En japonés, la cópula cortés que equivale a 'ser/estar' es 'です' (desu). Se ubica al final.
content.usage: Usage: Place at end of noun/adjective sentences. 本です。 (It is a book.)

content.text: In Japanese, the polite negative form of 'desu' (am not / is not / are not) is '{{c1::ではありません}}' (de wa arimasen) or casually '{{c2::じゃないです}}' (ja nai desu).
content.explanation: 'de wa arimasen' is formal written/spoken. 'ja nai desu' is common spoken.
content.spanish: En japonés, la forma negativa cortés de 'desu' es 'ではありません' (de wa arimasen) o 'じゃないです' (ja nai desu).
content.usage: Formal: 学生ではありません。 | Casual: 学生じゃないです。 (I am not a student.)

content.text: To make a question in Japanese, you add the question particle '{{c1::か}}' (ka) at the very end of the sentence.
content.explanation: Acts like a spoken question mark. You do not invert subject/verb or add auxiliary verbs.
content.spanish: En japonés, las preguntas se forman añadiendo la partícula 'か' (ka) al final de la oración.
content.usage: Example: 学生ですか？ (Are you a student?) | そうですか。 (Is that so?)

content.text: In Japanese, the topic marker particle is '{{c1::は}}' (pronounced 'wa'). Example: わたし{{c2::は}}学生です (As for me, I am a student).
content.explanation: Written with the character for 'ha' but pronounced as 'wa' when used as a particle.
content.spanish: En japonés, la partícula marcadora de tema es 'は' (se pronuncia 'wa').
content.usage: Rule: Subject/Topic + は + Predicate. Example: これは何ですか？ (What is this?)

content.text: In Japanese, the direct object marker particle is '{{c1::を}}' (pronounced 'o').
content.explanation: Written with 'wo' (を) but normally pronounced 'o'. It marks the target of transitive actions.
content.spanish: En japonés, la partícula marcadora de objeto directo es 'を' (se pronuncia 'o').
content.usage: Example: 水を飲みます。 (I drink water. [mizu = water, nomimasu = drink])

content.text: In Japanese, the possessive particle that acts like English apostrophe 's or 'of' is '{{c1::の}}' (no). Example: My book = わたし{{c2::の}}本.
content.explanation: Connects nouns. Noun 1 + の + Noun 2 -> Noun 2 belongs to Noun 1.
content.spanish: En japonés, la partícula posesiva o modificadora de sustantivos es 'の' (no). わたしの本 = Mi libro.
content.usage: Example: 日本のカメラ (Japanese camera) | 先生の本 (The teacher's book)

content.text: In Japanese, the words for 'this', 'that' (near listener), and 'that over there' (far from both) are '{{c1::これ}}' (Kore), '{{c2::それ}}' (Sore), and '{{c3::あれ}}' (Are).
content.explanation: These are demonstrative pronouns. The corresponding demonstrative adjectives (followed by a noun) are kono, sono, ano.
content.spanish: En japonés: 'これ' (esto), 'それ' (eso) y 'あれ' (aquello).
content.usage: Pronoun: これはペンです。 (This is a pen.) | Adjective: このペンはわたしのです。 (This pen is mine.)

content.text: In Japanese, what is the polite phrase to say 'My name is [Name]' using the copula? '[Name] {{c1::です}}' or '[Name] {{c2::と申します}}' (more formal).
content.explanation: Simply putting 'desu' after your name is the most common self-introduction. 'To moushimasu' translates to 'I am called...'.
content.spanish: Para decir 'Mi nombre es...' en japonés de forma sencilla: '[Nombre] です' o '[Nombre] と申します' (humilde/formal).
content.usage: Example: ジーザスです。 (I am Jesus.)

content.text: In Japanese, the particle meaning 'also' or 'too' is '{{c1::も}}' (mo), which replaces 'wa' or 'ga'. Example: Me too = わたし{{c2::も}}.
content.explanation: Identical in meaning to Spanish 'también'.
content.spanish: En japonés, la partícula que significa 'también' es 'も' (mo), la cual reemplaza a 'wa'.
content.usage: Example: 彼も学生です。 (He is also a student.)

content.text: In Japanese, the question word for 'What' is '{{c1::何}}' (pronounced 'nani' or 'nan').
content.explanation: 'nan' is used before 'desu' or counter words. 'nani' is used in other cases.
content.spanish: En japonés, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Qué?' es '何' (nani / nan).
content.usage: Examples: 何ですか？ (What is it?) | 何を食べますか？ (What will you eat?)

content.text: In Japanese, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::だれ}}' (Dare) or politely '{{c2::どなた}}' (Donata).
content.explanation: 'Donata' is used in business or formal situations.
content.spanish: En japonés, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Quién?' es 'だれ' (Dare) o formalmente 'どなた' (Donata).
content.usage: Example: あの人は誰ですか？ (Who is that person?)

content.text: In Japanese, what is the polite phrase meaning 'Nice to meet you' when exchanging names? {{c1::よろしくおねがいします}} (Yoroshiku onegaishimasu).
content.explanation: Literally: 'Please treat me well.' It's an indispensable greeting in self-introductions.
content.spanish: En japonés, la frase clave para cerrar una presentación y decir 'Un placer' es 'よろしくおねがいします'.
content.usage: Dialogue: - ジーザスです。どうぞよろしく。 (- I am Jesus. Nice to meet you.)
